title: High-Value Gaming Currency Dispute After In-Game Asset Transfer
narrative: Cardholder purchases $200-$500 of in-game currency or digital items (ProductCD=W for digital goods) from gaming platforms, completes the transaction with immediate delivery confirmation, then disputes 8-14 days later claiming card was stolen. Pattern shows legitimate cardholder behavior: matching P_emaildomain to previous transaction history, correct CVV, AVS full match, txn_hour consistent with account's historical gaming sessions (typically 18:00-23:00). The cardholder actively uses the purchased items in-game for 5-12 days before initiating chargeback with reason code 4837 (No Cardholder Authorization). Transaction shows card1_txn_count_24h of 1-2, normal for gaming purchases, TransactionAmt consistently in $249-$499 range, and dist1 under 50km. Device fingerprint matches prior approved transactions. The timing patternâ€”purchase during game event or expansion launch, dispute filed just before 15-day optimal window closesâ€”reveals intentional fraud rather than genuine unauthorized use.
indicators: TransactionAmt between $200-$500 for ProductCD=W with immediate digital delivery
Chargeback filed 8-14 days post-transaction with 4837 reason code
Perfect match on CVV, AVS, P_emaildomain consistent with 3+ prior transactions on same card
txn_hour falls within cardholder's historical active gaming window (Â±2 hours of previous sessions)
card1_txn_count_24h of 1-2, card1_amt_zscore under 1.5 (normal spending pattern)
dist1 under 50km from billing addr1, device fingerprint matches 2+ approved historical transactions
Purchase timestamp aligns with game event launch (within 48 hours), dispute filed day 8-14
summary: Digital gaming purchases ($200-$500) show perfect authentication matches—correct CVV, address, email domain, device fingerprint, and normal transaction timing—followed by chargebacks 8-14 days later claiming the card was stolen despite all legitimacy signals.
reasoning: The cardholder is exploiting the chargeback system to get free in-game items after already using them for over a week, timing the dispute to maximize their play time while still falling within the fraud claim window rather than reflecting genuine card theft.
indicator_explanations: Purchase is a mid-tier digital gaming item delivered instantly to the buyer
Dispute arrives in the classic friendly fraud window after item was consumed
All fraud checks passed perfectly matching the legitimate cardholder's verified information
Transaction occurred during the cardholder's typical gaming hours not anomalous timing
Spending volume and amount are completely normal for this cardholder's baseline
Purchase originated from cardholder's home location using their recognized trusted device
Buyer purchased right after new content dropped then disputed after using it
